summary: Gruber announces open sponsorship slots for both Daring Fireball's weekly website sponsorships and The Talk Show podcast. He reflects on the history of his sponsorship model since 2007, noting its success due to relevance and exclusivity. He describes upheaval in podcast advertising, where ad agencies displaced indie sponsors with startup brands, creating more hassle and now leaving gaps as that model contracts. He encourages small indie companies to sponsor The Talk Show directly, offering discounts to first-timers. He closes with self-deprecating honesty about his discomfort with self-promotion despite advertising being his actual business model.
key_points: Daring Fireball's single-sponsor-per-week model has worked since 2007 because it respects readers while delivering results for advertisers
Podcast advertising is going through upheaval, with the agency-driven startup brand era contracting and leaving indie podcasts struggling to fill spots
Gruber wants to shift The Talk Show's sponsorship back toward small indie companies dealing directly, rather than through ad agencies
High repeat-sponsor rates serve as the strongest evidence that the advertising actually works
Gruber candidly acknowledges he's functionally an ad salesman who doesn't promote his own inventory enough
money_quotes: Weekly sponsorships have been the top source of revenue for Daring Fireball ever since I started selling them back in 2007.

There's only one sponsor per week and the sponsors are always relevant to at least some sizable portion of the DF audience, so you, the reader, are never annoyed and hopefully often intrigued by them.
My favorite thing about them is how many sponsors return for subsequent weeks after seeing the results.
Advertising in the podcast industry is going through upheaval, and my show is really feeling it.

The brands and products were great — companies like Warby Parker eyeglasses, Casper mattresses, Hullo pillows, you know the type — but dealing with ad agencies was (and remains) a much, much bigger hassle than dealing with smaller independent companies directly.
Small indie products have been vastly underrepresented amongst The Talk Show sponsors in recent years, but I hope to change that.
I kind of hate writing these sponsorship-pimping posts, but my job, from an accounting point of view, is an ad salesman, not a writer/podcaster.
I'd have been fired from this sales job years ago if I had a boss.
key_insight: The podcast advertising boom built on agency-placed startup brands is contracting, and indie podcasters need to return to direct relationships with smaller sponsors to sustain their businesses.
